title: Chapter 13: The Green Shadow
paragraphs: Long before the heavens were torn asunder by portals and the world began to choke on its own ash, there was Yashil.

He was born in a burrow. Not a cave—a burrow. In a damp hole, smelling of rot and sour earth, dug into the slope of a ravine on the outskirts of Rotten Hollow—the most despised goblin settlement east of Agdash. The burrow was so cramped that an adult goblin could not stand upright in it. The ceiling was the roots of a dead tree, the walls were packed clay, and the floor was the same earth upon which his mother laid a bundle of damp straw and called it a cradle.

Yashil's mother died when he was four winters old. She did not perish in battle, nor fall to magic—she simply stopped eating, giving her last crumbs to her son. When Yashil woke up one morning and reached for her hand, it was as cold as the stone they slept on. He did not cry. Goblins do not cry—they are not allowed to.

"Your mother croaked because she was weak," said Gnaw, the elder of Rotten Hollow, spitting yellow saliva onto the ground beside the body. "Remember that. In this world, only the one who takes survives. Giving is for fools and the dead."
Yashil remembered. But not in the way Gnaw had intended.
He remembered that his mother had given everything she had, and that there was not a trace of weakness in it. It was the strongest thing he had ever seen in his life.

Rotten Hollow existed on the fringes of civilization, like a parasite on the body of a sick beast. A hundred burrows, chaotically dug into the clay slope, connected by winding paths covered in refuse. Goblins lived here not because they chose this place, but because they were not allowed anywhere else.

The laws of the elven cities were simple and absolute: goblins forbidden. Not recommended against. Not unwelcome. Forbidden. Guards had every right to kill any green-skinned creature who crossed the line of the city gates. And they exercised that right with pleasure.
"Why can't we go there?" five-year-old Yashil asked old Murka, who looked after the Hollow's young while the others scavenged in the slag heaps.
Murka looked at him with her one remaining eye. An elven soldier had knocked the other out twenty years ago when she had accidentally wandered onto a trade route.

"Because we are goblins, little one," she replied with a tired smile that held neither anger nor bitterness. Only the resignation of a creature that had accepted its fate as a law of nature. "To them, we are worse than rats. At least no one goes looking for a rat to kill it for sport."
"But we didn't do anything wrong."
"We are goblins," Murka repeated, as if that explained everything.
And for this world, it did.
By seven winters, Yashil had learned several things that would define his entire future.

First, he learned to observe. While other goblin children fought over scraps and learned to steal, Yashil would sit for hours on the edge of the ravine, watching the road. Caravans passed by: elves in gleaming armor, dwarves with heavy wagons, sometimes tall, fair-skinned beings with an aura so pure they smelled of morning rain. Yashil did not envy them. He studied them.

Second, he realized that the world was arranged unfairly, not because it had to be, but because those who were stronger had decided it so. And that could be changed. Not by force—goblins had no force. But by something else. Something that no one had yet taken from them.
"Kindness," he whispered one night, looking at the dim stars through the cracks in his burrow's ceiling. "If I do good, they will see that we are not monsters."
These words, spoken by a small green creature in a stinking pit on the outskirts of a dying world, were laughably naive. So naive that they contained an absolute, indestructible truth.
The first time Yashil tried to help a traveler was at eight years old.

An elven merchant, traveling from the southern outposts, had broken a wheel on his wagon on the rutted road. Yashil heard the cursing from the bushes and cautiously crawled closer. The elf was alone, unguarded, and desperately trying to fit a heavy wooden rim back onto the axle.
The small goblin silently stepped out of the thicket and held out a smooth stone that could serve as a prop.
The merchant turned around.
A second of silence.
Then a scream, full of such animalistic, irrational disgust that Yashil remembered it for his entire life.
"Out! Away from me, beast!" The elf threw the stone aside and swung a whip. The blow cut the air a centimeter from Yashil's face. "Get out, before I call the guard! Vile freak!"

Yashil ran. He ran through the thorny brush, tearing the skin on his arms and knees, until he scrambled back into his burrow. There, in the dark, he lay for a long time, curled in a ball, clutching his hands to his chest where bright green goblin blood was seeping out.
He did not cry. Goblins do not cry.

But something inside him, fragile and bright, was covered in its first crack that day. And yet—it did not break. Because Yashil looked at his wounded hands and thought not "why?", but "next time I will be more careful."
That was where his greatness lay. Not in magic, not in strength, not in agility. In the absolute, unwavering refusal to become embittered.
